title: Maternal Refusal of Recommended Cesarean Birth for Category III Fetal Heart Tracing (Recurrent Late Decelerations + Minimal Variability) in a 28-Year-Old G2P1 at 39 Weeks in Active Labor: Respecting the Informed Refusal of a Decisional, Competent Pregnant Adult While Maximizing Intrauterine Resuscitation, Ensuring Truly Informed Consent/Refusal, Activating an Ethics Consult, Preserving the Therapeutic Alliance, and Avoiding Coercion
subtitle: ETHICAL + LEGAL PRINCIPLE — a DECISIONAL (competent) adult, INCLUDING a pregnant woman, has the right to make informed decisions about her own body and to REFUSE recommended medical or surgical interventions, even when refusal poses risk to herself or the fetus; AUTONOMY + bodily integrity + informed consent are foundational; FORCED/COURT-ORDERED CESAREANS are ethically condemned by major bodies (ACOG strongly opposes coercion, threats, manipulation, or court intervention to override a competent pregnant patient's refusal) and are legally disfavored — the fetus does not have legal personhood that overrides the mother's bodily autonomy in this context, and forcing surgery on a competent adult constitutes battery; the nurse's role is ADVOCACY + ensuring the refusal is TRULY INFORMED (the patient understands the recommendation, the risks/benefits/alternatives, and the realistic consequences to fetus and self), NOT coercion or abandonment; INFORMED CONSENT/REFUSAL elements = (1) the patient has DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY (understands relevant information + appreciates how it applies to her + reasons through options + communicates a stable choice; capacity is decision-specific + presumed in adults; labor pain, fear, or disagreement with providers does NOT by itself negate capacity; assess for impairments — intoxication, severe psychiatric illness, hypoxia, coercion); (2) DISCLOSURE of the diagnosis, recommended intervention, risks/benefits, alternatives, and consequences of refusal in understandable language (professional interpreter if needed — never family/staff ad hoc); (3) VOLUNTARINESS (free from coercion/manipulation); (4) the choice is documented. CLINICAL CONTEXT — CATEGORY III FETAL HEART RATE TRACING (NICHD 3-tier system): Category I = normal (baseline 110-160, moderate variability, no late/variable decels, ± early decels/accelerations) → continue routine; Category II = indeterminate (the broad middle — e.g., tachycardia, minimal variability, recurrent variable decels, prolonged decels) → requires evaluation + intrauterine resuscitation + surveillance; Category III = ABNORMAL/predictive of abnormal fetal acid-base status = ABSENT baseline variability WITH recurrent late decelerations, OR recurrent variable decelerations, OR bradycardia, OR a SINUSOIDAL pattern → requires PROMPT intervention (intrauterine resuscitation) and, if not rapidly resolved, EXPEDITED DELIVERY (often cesarean). RECURRENT LATE DECELERATIONS (gradual decrease in FHR beginning after the contraction peak, recurring with ≥50% of contractions) reflect UTEROPLACENTAL INSUFFICIENCY/fetal hypoxemia. INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION (measures to improve fetal oxygenation that this patient WILL accept) = (a) maternal REPOSITIONING (lateral/left side to relieve aortocaval compression + improve uteroplacental flow; or hands-knees); (b) STOP/REDUCE oxytocin + treat tachysystole (consider terbutaline tocolysis); (c) IV FLUID BOLUS for hypotension/hypovolemia; (d) treat maternal HYPOTENSION (e.g., after epidural — fluids ± ephedrine/phenylephrine); (e) maternal OXYGEN (10 L/min non-rebreather — use is debated/limited-evidence but sometimes applied for Category III); (f) vaginal exam to assess rapid progress/cord prolapse; (g) consider amnioinfusion for recurrent variable decels. NURSING RESPONSE TO REFUSAL = stay present + non-judgmental + therapeutic communication; ensure the conversation is truly informed (clear, repeated, interpreter as needed); explore the REASONS behind refusal (prior birth trauma, fear of surgery, cultural/religious beliefs, mistrust, misunderstanding) — addressing the underlying concern sometimes resolves the impasse; offer acceptable alternatives + continue intrauterine resuscitation + continuous monitoring; involve the OB, ethics committee/consult, chaplain, social work, and the patient's support person; document the capacity assessment, the information disclosed, the patient's stated understanding and decision, and the ongoing dialogue; NEVER coerce, threaten (e.g., with CPS/court), shame, or abandon the patient; respect that she may change her mind at any time and keep the door open; if the patient ultimately consents, proceed; if she continues to refuse and remains decisional, her refusal is honored while care continues. DISTINGUISH from situations where intervention may proceed without consent — a true EMERGENCY in a patient who has LOST capacity (e.g., unconscious) may invoke implied/emergency consent or a surrogate; and the standard for OVERRIDING parental refusal for a CHILD (best-interest, court-orderable — see [[case-080]]) is DIFFERENT from refusal by a competent ADULT for her own body. KEY CONTRASTS (wrong actions) = obtaining a court order to force cesarean on a competent refusing adult; physically restraining/coercing; threatening child-welfare referral to pressure her; declaring her incompetent solely because she disagrees; abandoning or punishing her

patient.name: D. Achebe
patient.code: Full code; DECISIONAL adult with intact decision-making capacity; her own legal decision-maker for her body; husband present as support person (not a surrogate while she retains capacity); goals = safe birth + to be heard/respected + avoid surgery if at all possible due to prior traumatic cesarean experience
patient.admitDate: Admitted in spontaneous active labor at 39+1 weeks (G2P1; one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE CESAREAN 3 years ago for breech, complicated by a difficult recovery + wound infection + what she describes as a frightening, poorly-communicated experience — strongly desiring a VBAC this time and counseled as an appropriate TOLAC candidate); progressed to 7 cm with reassuring Category I tracing initially; over the last 40 minutes the fetal heart tracing evolved to CATEGORY III — recurrent LATE decelerations with MINIMAL/ABSENT baseline variability (baseline 150) — concerning for uteroplacental insufficiency/fetal hypoxemia; OB recommends EXPEDITED CESAREAN birth; patient, who is alert + oriented + reasoning clearly between contractions, states she understands the concern but is REFUSING cesarean at this time, asking to continue intrauterine resuscitation + labor + reassessment because of her prior traumatic surgical experience; intrauterine resuscitation measures initiated; ethics + multidisciplinary engagement activated
patient.room: Labor & Delivery Room 4 (continuous EFM; OR on standby; husband at bedside; OB + charge RN + anesthesia + neonatal team + ethics consult + chaplain + social work engaged; calm, private, non-coercive environment maintained)

vitals.temp: 37.1°C; mother alert + oriented x4 + conversant + reasoning clearly between contractions; 7 cm / 100% / -1 station; FETAL: baseline 150, MINIMAL-to-ABSENT variability, RECURRENT LATE decelerations with ≥50% of contractions (Category III); contractions q2-3 min, some tachysystole on oxytocin; OB recommends expedited cesarean; patient verbalizes understanding of the concern but is refusing cesarean at this moment

37.1°C; INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION started (measures the patient accepts): repositioned to LEFT LATERAL then hands-knees; OXYTOCIN STOPPED; IV LACTATED RINGER'S bolus; maternal OXYGEN 10 L/min via non-rebreother offered + applied; vaginal exam to assess progress + rule out cord prolapse (no prolapse; 7-8 cm); terbutaline considered for tachysystole; continuous EFM maintained; OB at bedside; calm explanation provided

37.1°C; FETAL: with intrauterine resuscitation, variability intermittently improving to minimal-moderate, late decels persisting but less deep; CAPACITY ASSESSMENT by OB + RN: patient understands the diagnosis (the baby is showing signs of stress), the recommendation (cesarean), the risks of refusal (possible fetal hypoxic injury or death), and the alternatives — she can paraphrase the information accurately + is reasoning + choice stable = HAS CAPACITY; refusal explored — rooted in PRIOR TRAUMATIC CESAREAN + fear; ethics consult + chaplain + social work engaged; husband supportive of her right to choose; non-coercive therapeutic dialogue continues; OR remains on standby

37.0°C; ongoing therapeutic communication — OB calmly re-explains, addresses her trauma + fear, commits to a supportive, well-communicated surgical experience if needed (continuous narration, her husband present, doula/chaplain, regional anesthesia, gentle/family-centered cesarean) ; patient feels HEARD; FETAL: late decels recurring, variability minimal; OB explains that if the tracing does not improve, the safest option for the baby is cesarean; patient asks thoughtful questions; ethics consultant affirms her autonomy + supports the team's non-coercive informational approach

37.0°C; after feeling genuinely heard + having her fears addressed + understanding the persistent Category III tracing, the patient VOLUNTARILY CONSENTS to cesarean; she states she trusts the team now that she has been listened to; informed CONSENT obtained + documented; consent NOT coerced — she changed her mind through dialogue + addressing her trauma; preparing for cesarean with family-centered, trauma-informed approach (husband present, narration, chaplain, gentle technique)

37.0°C; cesarean birth completed; NEWBORN male, Apgar 6 (1 min) → 8 (5 min) → 9 (10 min); cord arterial pH 7.18 (mild acidemia, improving); brief PPV then vigorous; skin-to-skin with mother in OR/recovery; breastfeeding initiated; mother recovering well + hemostatic + bonded + reports feeling respected + that the experience was different/positive this time; debrief offered; trauma-informed postpartum follow-up planned

labs.n: PRESENTATION + obstetric history + the ethical/clinical situation
labs.v: 28 y/o woman D. Achebe, G2P1, admitted in spontaneous ACTIVE LABOR at 39+1 weeks. OBSTETRIC HISTORY: one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE CESAREAN 3 years ago (performed for breech presentation) complicated by a difficult recovery + surgical-site infection + a subjectively FRIGHTENING, POORLY-COMMUNICATED experience (she felt unheard, was not told what was happening, and developed lasting fear of surgery — a form of birth trauma). She strongly desired a VAGINAL BIRTH AFTER CESAREAN (VBAC) this pregnancy and was appropriately counseled as a candidate for a TRIAL OF LABOR AFTER CESAREAN (TOLAC) — one prior low-transverse incision, no contraindications, adequate counseling + informed consent for TOLAC including uterine-rupture risk (~0.5-1%). Current labor progressed normally to 7 cm with a reassuring CATEGORY I tracing initially. PMH: healthy; no chronic conditions; no psychiatric diagnosis; not intoxicated; English-proficient (also speaks Igbo; interpreter available); no cognitive impairment. PRENATAL care complete + uncomplicated pregnancy; GBS negative; appropriate weight gain. SOCIAL: married 5 years; husband (30, supportive, present at bedside) and a 3-year-old at home; works as a paralegal; engaged, informed, asks good questions; cultural background Nigerian-American; Christian faith; values being a partner in her care after feeling powerless last time. THE SITUATION: over ~40 minutes the fetal heart tracing evolved from Category I to CATEGORY III — recurrent LATE decelerations with MINIMAL-to-ABSENT baseline variability (baseline 150) — a pattern predictive of fetal hypoxemia/abnormal acid-base status from uteroplacental insufficiency; there was tachysystole on oxytocin. The OB recommends EXPEDITED CESAREAN. The patient — ALERT, ORIENTED, conversant, and reasoning clearly between contractions — states she UNDERSTANDS the concern but is REFUSING cesarean at this moment, asking to continue intrauterine resuscitation and labor with reassessment, citing her prior traumatic surgical experience and fear. This creates an ETHICAL situation: balancing respect for the autonomy/informed refusal of a competent pregnant adult against beneficence toward the fetus — with the clear ethical/legal principle that a competent adult's informed refusal of surgery on her own body must be respected, and coercion/forced surgery/court orders are not appropriate.
labs.r: A decisional, competent pregnant adult is refusing a recommended cesarean for a Category III tracing. The situation requires intrauterine resuscitation + a careful capacity assessment + truly informed consent/refusal + advocacy + ethics engagement + non-coercive therapeutic communication. RN role: recognize the clinical urgency AND protect the patient's autonomy + dignity.

labs.n: Fetal heart rate categories + Category III interpretation
labs.v: NICHD THREE-TIER FETAL HEART RATE (FHR) CLASSIFICATION: CATEGORY I (NORMAL) = baseline 110-160 bpm + MODERATE variability (6-25 bpm) + NO late or variable decelerations + ± early decelerations + ± accelerations → strongly predictive of normal fetal acid-base status → routine care. CATEGORY II (INDETERMINATE) = the large middle group not Category I or III — e.g., tachycardia, bradycardia with variability present, minimal variability, absent variability without recurrent decels, recurrent variable decels, prolonged decels, marked variability → NOT predictive of abnormal acid-base status but requires evaluation + surveillance + intrauterine resuscitation + reassessment. CATEGORY III (ABNORMAL) = predictive of abnormal fetal acid-base status at the time of observation → either (a) ABSENT baseline variability WITH any of: recurrent LATE decelerations, recurrent VARIABLE decelerations, or BRADYCARDIA; OR (b) a SINUSOIDAL pattern → requires PROMPT intervention: intrauterine resuscitation and, if not rapidly resolved, EXPEDITED DELIVERY (commonly cesarean if vaginal birth not imminent). DECELERATION TYPES: EARLY (mirror the contraction; gradual; from head compression; benign); VARIABLE (abrupt drop; from cord compression; V/W-shaped; significance depends on depth/duration/repetition); LATE (gradual decrease beginning AFTER the contraction peak with nadir after the peak; from UTEROPLACENTAL INSUFFICIENCY/fetal hypoxemia; RECURRENT late decels = concerning, especially with reduced variability). VARIABILITY (fluctuations in baseline) is a key indicator of fetal oxygenation/autonomic function: moderate = reassuring; minimal/absent = concerning (hypoxia, acidosis, sleep cycle, meds, anomalies). THIS PATIENT: baseline 150 + MINIMAL-to-ABSENT variability + RECURRENT LATE decelerations = CATEGORY III → urgent intrauterine resuscitation + consideration of expedited delivery. ADJUNCTS if persistent + delivery delayed/declined: scalp stimulation (acceleration suggests pH >7.20), reassess after resuscitation; the clinical urgency is real and must be communicated clearly to the patient as part of informed consent/refusal.
labs.r: Category III FHR (absent variability with recurrent late decels, here) predicts abnormal fetal acid-base status → prompt intrauterine resuscitation + expedited delivery if unresolved. Recurrent LATE decels = uteroplacental insufficiency. This clinical urgency must be clearly conveyed to the patient as part of informed decision-making. RN role: accurate FHR interpretation + escalation + intrauterine resuscitation.

labs.v: INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION = nursing/clinical measures to improve fetal oxygenation in response to a concerning (Category II/III) tracing, buying time and sometimes resolving the pattern; importantly, these are interventions the refusing patient WILL accept and should be maximized: (1) MATERNAL REPOSITIONING — turn to LEFT LATERAL (or right lateral, or hands-and-knees) to relieve AORTOCAVAL COMPRESSION (gravid uterus on the inferior vena cava/aorta) and improve uteroplacental perfusion + relieve cord compression; first-line, immediate. (2) REDUCE/STOP UTEROTONICS — STOP oxytocin (and remove a dinoprostone insert) to reduce uterine activity; treat TACHYSYSTOLE (>5 contractions/10 min averaged over 30 min) which reduces placental perfusion; consider a TOCOLYTIC (terbutaline 0.25 mg SC) for tachysystole with a Category III tracing. (3) IV FLUID BOLUS — a bolus of isotonic crystalloid (lactated Ringer's/normal saline) to expand intravascular volume + improve uteroplacental flow, especially with hypotension/hypovolemia. (4) TREAT MATERNAL HYPOTENSION — common after neuraxial (epidural/spinal) anesthesia → IV fluids + vasopressor (ephedrine or phenylephrine) + positioning; restoring maternal BP restores placental perfusion. (5) MATERNAL OXYGEN — 10 L/min via non-rebreather mask has traditionally been used for Category III patterns, though recent evidence questions benefit for routine Category II; reasonable as part of resuscitation for Category III. (6) VAGINAL EXAM — assess for rapid cervical change/imminent birth and RULE OUT UMBILICAL CORD PROLAPSE (a cause of acute bradycardia requiring elevation of the presenting part + emergent delivery) and assess for rapid descent. (7) AMNIOINFUSION — for recurrent VARIABLE decelerations from cord compression/oligohydramnios. (8) Reassess the tracing after measures; if Category III persists, expedited delivery is indicated. THIS PATIENT received repositioning, oxytocin discontinuation, IV LR bolus, oxygen 10 L/min, and a vaginal exam (no cord prolapse), with intermittent improvement in variability while late decels persisted — providing time for the informed-consent dialogue to continue safely while keeping the OR on standby.
labs.r: Intrauterine resuscitation = reposition (left lateral/hands-knees), stop oxytocin/treat tachysystole (± terbutaline), IV fluid bolus, treat hypotension, maternal O2, vaginal exam (rule out cord prolapse), amnioinfusion for variables. Maximize these acceptable measures while delivery decisions are made. RN role: rapidly implement + reassess + keep OR ready.

labs.v: INFORMED CONSENT/REFUSAL is both an ethical duty (respecting autonomy) and a legal requirement; its ELEMENTS: (1) DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY — the patient can (a) UNDERSTAND the relevant information, (b) APPRECIATE how it applies to her own situation, (c) REASON/weigh the options and consequences, and (d) COMMUNICATE a stable choice. Capacity is DECISION-SPECIFIC (can have capacity for one decision and not another) and is PRESUMED in adults. Critically: disagreeing with the medical recommendation, being in pain, being frightened, or making a choice the team considers unwise does NOT by itself indicate lack of capacity. Assess for genuine impairments to capacity (intoxication, delirium, hypoxia, acute psychosis, severe cognitive impairment, coercion). 'Competence' is a legal term (court-determined); clinicians assess 'capacity.' (2) DISCLOSURE — the diagnosis, the recommended intervention, its risks/benefits, the reasonable ALTERNATIVES, and the CONSEQUENCES of refusal, communicated in language the patient understands (use a PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER if there is any language barrier — never family members or ad hoc staff). (3) VOLUNTARINESS — the decision is free from coercion, manipulation, threats, or undue influence. (4) DOCUMENTATION — the capacity assessment, information disclosed, the patient's demonstrated understanding, and the decision. THE COMPETENT PREGNANT ADULT'S RIGHT TO REFUSE: a decisional pregnant woman has the same right as any competent adult to refuse interventions on her own body, even surgery recommended for fetal benefit. ACOG and bioethics consensus: coercion, manipulation, threats, deception, and especially COURT-ORDERED/FORCED CESAREANS are unethical and counterproductive; the fetus's interests do not legally override the mother's bodily autonomy; forcing surgery on a competent adult is battery and erodes trust. The appropriate response is repeated, clear, compassionate INFORMATION + addressing the patient's underlying concerns + offering acceptable alternatives + time + ethics consultation — not force. CONTRAST: (a) EMERGENCY/IMPLIED CONSENT applies when a patient has LOST capacity (e.g., unconscious) and no surrogate is immediately available and delay would cause serious harm — not applicable to a conscious, decisional patient who is actively refusing; (b) overriding a PARENT's refusal of life-saving treatment for a CHILD uses the best-interest standard and can involve court orders (see [[case-080]]) — this is DIFFERENT from a competent adult refusing for her OWN body. THIS PATIENT was assessed to HAVE capacity (accurately paraphrased the diagnosis/recommendation/risks/alternatives, reasoned, stable choice, no impairment) — so her refusal had to be respected even as the team continued to inform and support her.
labs.r: Informed consent/refusal requires CAPACITY (understand + appreciate + reason + communicate; presumed in adults; disagreement/pain/fear ≠ incapacity) + disclosure (with professional interpreter) + voluntariness + documentation. A competent pregnant adult may refuse cesarean for her own body; coercion/forced/court-ordered cesarean is unethical (ACOG) + battery. RN role: assess capacity, ensure informed dialogue, advocate, document.

labs.n: Nursing role — advocacy, therapeutic communication, ethics process, documentation
labs.v: NURSING RESPONSE to a competent patient's refusal of a recommended intervention: (1) STAY PRESENT + NON-JUDGMENTAL — do not abandon, shame, punish, or withdraw warmth; the therapeutic relationship is the most powerful tool. (2) ENSURE THE REFUSAL IS TRULY INFORMED — verify she understands the diagnosis, recommendation, risks/benefits, alternatives, and consequences; re-explain clearly and as many times as needed; use a PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER for any language barrier; correct misunderstandings. (3) EXPLORE THE 'WHY' — refusals are often driven by fear, prior trauma (as here — a previous frightening, poorly-communicated cesarean), mistrust, cultural/religious beliefs, misinformation, or feeling unheard; eliciting and addressing the underlying concern (e.g., committing to narration, her support person's presence, a gentle/family-centered cesarean, regional anesthesia, chaplain/doula support) frequently resolves the impasse without any coercion. (4) MAXIMIZE ACCEPTABLE CARE — continue intrauterine resuscitation + continuous fetal monitoring + keep the OR ready; offer everything she will accept. (5) MOBILIZE THE TEAM + ETHICS PROCESS — involve the OB, charge nurse, anesthesia, neonatology, the patient's support person, chaplain, social work, and request an ETHICS CONSULT/committee (an ethics consult supports the patient's autonomy and the team's process — it is NOT a tool to override her). (6) AVOID COERCION — no threats (including threatening child-welfare/CPS referral or court action), no manipulation, no deception, no physical force; these are unethical and destroy trust. (7) RESPECT REVERSIBILITY — keep the door open; she may change her mind at any moment (as this patient did, voluntarily, after feeling heard); if she consents, proceed promptly with a trauma-informed approach; if she continues to refuse and remains decisional, honor it while continuing supportive care. (8) DOCUMENT objectively — capacity assessment, information disclosed, interpreter use, the patient's stated understanding and decision, intrauterine resuscitation provided, team/ethics involvement, and the ongoing dialogue; document facts and the patient's own words, not judgments. (9) SELF-AWARENESS — providers may feel moral distress when an autonomous choice risks the fetus; debrief + support the team; distress does not justify coercion. NEWBORN/POSTPARTUM: if delivery occurs, support bonding (skin-to-skin), breastfeeding, and trauma-informed postpartum care + debriefing to repair the birth experience. The aim throughout: protect BOTH the patient's autonomy/dignity AND fetal wellbeing through information, trust, and support — not force.
labs.r: Nursing role = presence + non-judgment + ensuring truly informed refusal (interpreter, re-explain) + exploring/addressing the underlying reason (often fear/trauma) + maximizing acceptable care + activating ethics consult + AVOIDING all coercion/threats + keeping the door open + objective documentation + team support. Trust and information — not force — protect both patient and fetus.

labs.v: PERTINENT NEGATIVES (capacity + safety): NO impairment of decision-making capacity (not intoxicated + no delirium + no hypoxia + no psychiatric incapacity + no cognitive impairment + accurately paraphrases information + reasons + stable choice — she HAS capacity; her refusal is informed, not a product of impairment); NO language barrier left unaddressed (English-proficient + professional interpreter available for Igbo if needed — never family/ad hoc); NO coercion applied by the team (no threats, no court action, no CPS threat, no manipulation, no physical force, no abandonment); NO declaration of incapacity merely because she disagreed; NO umbilical cord prolapse (vaginal exam negative — a cause of acute bradycardia ruled out); NO uterine rupture signs during TOLAC (no acute severe pain/loss of station/hematuria/maternal instability — important given prior cesarean); NO maternal hemodynamic instability; NO maternal hypoxia. NEGATIVES that distinguish this from other consent scenarios: this is NOT a child (the best-interest/court-order standard for overriding a PARENT's refusal — see [[case-080]] — does not apply to a competent adult refusing for her own body); this is NOT an unconscious patient (emergency/implied consent does not apply to a conscious, decisional, actively-refusing patient). PROTECTIVE FACTORS: PROMPT recognition of the Category III tracing + immediate intrauterine resuscitation (repositioning + stop oxytocin + IV bolus + O2 + ruled out cord prolapse) bought time + intermittently improved variability; SKILLED, NON-COERCIVE therapeutic communication that elicited and ADDRESSED the patient's underlying fear/trauma; ETHICS CONSULT + chaplain + social work + supportive husband; the team's commitment to a trauma-informed, family-centered, well-communicated experience; the patient ultimately reached an autonomous, voluntary decision to consent after feeling heard — leading to timely cesarean with a good newborn outcome (Apgar 6→8→9, cord pH 7.18) and a mother who felt respected; trauma-informed postpartum follow-up + debriefing to repair her birth experience. The case models that respecting autonomy and providing excellent clinical care are NOT mutually exclusive — trust, not force, achieved both maternal dignity and fetal safety.
labs.r: Key negatives: capacity intact (refusal informed, not impaired), no coercion applied, cord prolapse + uterine rupture ruled out. Protective factors: prompt intrauterine resuscitation + skilled non-coercive communication addressing her trauma + ethics/chaplain/social-work support → patient voluntarily consented → good maternal + newborn outcome. Autonomy and excellent care coexist; trust, not force, achieved both.

nursesNote: 1420 — 28 y/o G2P1 at 39+1 wk in active labor (admitted spontaneous; progressed to 7 cm / 100% / -1 with initially reassuring Category I tracing). OB HISTORY: one prior LOW-TRANSVERSE CESAREAN 3 yr ago (for breech) complicated by wound infection + a FRIGHTENING, POORLY-COMMUNICATED experience leaving lasting fear of surgery (birth trauma); strongly desired VBAC + appropriately counseled/consented for TOLAC (one low-transverse incision, no contraindications, uterine-rupture risk discussed). PMH healthy; no psych dx; not intoxicated; English-proficient (also Igbo; interpreter available); no cognitive impairment; prenatal care complete; GBS negative. SOCIAL: married 5 yr, supportive husband at bedside + 3-yr-old at home; paralegal; engaged + informed; Nigerian-American; Christian; values being a partner in her care after feeling powerless last time. SITUATION: over ~40 min the FHR evolved Category I → CATEGORY III — recurrent LATE decelerations + MINIMAL-to-ABSENT variability (baseline 150) — concerning for uteroplacental insufficiency/fetal hypoxemia; tachysystole on oxytocin. OB recommends EXPEDITED CESAREAN. Patient — ALERT + oriented + conversant + reasoning clearly between contractions — verbalizes understanding of the concern but REFUSES cesarean at this moment, requesting continued intrauterine resuscitation + labor + reassessment, citing her prior traumatic surgery + fear. 1425 — INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION (measures she accepts): repositioned LEFT LATERAL then hands-knees; OXYTOCIN STOPPED; IV LACTATED RINGER'S bolus; maternal OXYGEN 10 L/min non-rebreather; vaginal exam → NO cord prolapse (7-8 cm); terbutaline considered for tachysystole; continuous EFM; OB at bedside; calm explanation. 1435 — variability intermittently improving to minimal-moderate with resuscitation; late decels less deep but persisting. CAPACITY ASSESSMENT (OB + RN): patient accurately paraphrases the diagnosis (baby showing stress), recommendation (cesarean), risks of refusal (possible fetal hypoxic injury/death), and alternatives; reasons through it; stable choice; no impairment → HAS CAPACITY → informed refusal must be respected. Refusal explored — rooted in prior traumatic cesarean + fear of being unheard again. ETHICS CONSULT + chaplain + social work engaged; husband supports her right to choose; NON-COERCIVE therapeutic dialogue continues; OR on standby. NO threats/coercion/CPS or court threats/physical force/abandonment used. 1450 — ongoing therapeutic communication: OB calmly re-explains, validates her trauma, and COMMITS to a supportive, well-communicated, trauma-informed/family-centered cesarean if needed (continuous narration, husband present, chaplain/doula, regional anesthesia, gentle technique); patient feels HEARD + asks thoughtful questions; ethics consultant affirms her autonomy + supports the team's informational, non-coercive approach; late decels recurring, variability minimal — OB explains that if the tracing does not improve, cesarean is the safest option for the baby. 1505 — after feeling genuinely heard + having her fears addressed + understanding the persistent Category III tracing, the patient VOLUNTARILY CONSENTS to cesarean; states she trusts the team now; informed CONSENT obtained + documented (NOT coerced — she changed her mind through dialogue). Prepared for cesarean with trauma-informed, family-centered approach. 1540 — CESAREAN completed; NEWBORN male Apgar 6→8→9, cord arterial pH 7.18 (mild acidemia improving), brief PPV then vigorous; SKIN-TO-SKIN + breastfeeding initiated; mother recovering well + hemostatic + bonded + reports feeling RESPECTED + that this experience was positive/different; debrief offered; trauma-informed postpartum follow-up planned. Documentation includes capacity assessment, information disclosed, intrauterine resuscitation, team/ethics involvement, the patient's own words, and the decision — facts not judgments.
providerOrders: ADMIT/CONTINUE: Labor & Delivery, active labor at 39+1 wk, G2P1, TOLAC in progress

DIAGNOSIS: Category III fetal heart tracing (recurrent late decelerations + minimal/absent variability) in active labor; maternal informed refusal of recommended cesarean by a decisional adult; trauma-informed care; subsequent voluntary consent
CODE STATUS: full code; patient is her own decision-maker (decisional adult); husband = support person (not surrogate while she retains capacity)
FETAL/LABOR MONITORING:
Continuous electronic fetal monitoring; document FHR category + decelerations + variability + contraction pattern
Reassess tracing continuously + after each intervention
Notify OB immediately of Category III features or any acute bradycardia/rupture signs
INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION (implement promptly; offer all the patient accepts):
Maternal repositioning — left lateral / right lateral / hands-and-knees
STOP oxytocin; treat tachysystole; consider terbutaline 0.25 mg SC for tachysystole with Category III
IV fluid bolus (lactated Ringer's)
Treat maternal hypotension (fluids ± ephedrine/phenylephrine), especially post-neuraxial
Maternal oxygen 10 L/min via non-rebreather (for Category III)
Vaginal exam — assess progress + RULE OUT cord prolapse
Amnioinfusion if recurrent variable decelerations
Keep OR + anesthesia + neonatal team on standby for expedited cesarean
INFORMED CONSENT/REFUSAL + CAPACITY:
Assess decision-making capacity (understand + appreciate + reason + communicate; presumed in adults; disagreement/pain/fear ≠ incapacity); document
Disclose diagnosis, recommendation, risks/benefits, alternatives, consequences of refusal in understandable terms
Use PROFESSIONAL INTERPRETER for any language barrier (never family/ad hoc staff)
Ensure voluntariness; document understanding + decision + the patient's own words
Respect the informed refusal of a decisional adult for surgery on her own body; she may reconsider at any time
NURSING ADVOCACY + THERAPEUTIC APPROACH:
Stay present + non-judgmental; do not abandon, shame, threaten, or coerce
Explore + address the underlying reason for refusal (prior birth trauma/fear/mistrust/beliefs)
Offer trauma-informed, family-centered options (narration, support person present, chaplain/doula, regional anesthesia, gentle/family-centered cesarean)
Maximize acceptable care + continuous monitoring; keep door open to reconsideration
PROHIBITED (do NOT do):
No coercion, manipulation, deception, or threats (including CPS/child-welfare or court threats)
No court order to force cesarean on a competent refusing adult; no physical restraint/forced surgery (= battery)
Do not declare incapacity solely because the patient disagrees with the recommendation
MULTIDISCIPLINARY + ETHICS:
OB + charge RN + anesthesia + neonatology + nursing leadership
ETHICS CONSULT/committee (support autonomy + team process; not to override the patient)
Chaplain + social work + interpreter services + doula if available
Risk management/patient advocate as appropriate; team debrief for moral distress
IF CONSENT OBTAINED (as occurred):
Proceed with timely trauma-informed, family-centered cesarean (narration, husband present, regional anesthesia, gentle technique)
Neonatal team present; newborn resuscitation per NRP; cord gases
Skin-to-skin + early breastfeeding as condition allows
POSTPARTUM:
Trauma-informed postpartum care + birth-experience debriefing to repair prior + current trauma
Support bonding + lactation; assess mood; postpartum follow-up
Document objectively throughout (capacity, disclosure, interventions, ethics involvement, decision)

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters in this refusal-of-cesarean situation?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings that are clinically significant cues requiring nursing attention.

opts.text: CATEGORY III fetal heart tracing — recurrent LATE decelerations with MINIMAL/ABSENT baseline variability (baseline 150) — predictive of abnormal fetal acid-base status (uteroplacental insufficiency) requiring prompt intrauterine resuscitation and, if unresolved, expedited delivery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — Category III is a true clinical urgency. RN role: accurate interpretation + intrauterine resuscitation + escalation.

opts.text: The patient is a DECISIONAL adult who UNDERSTANDS the recommendation but is REFUSING cesarean — a competent pregnant adult has the right to make informed decisions about her own body, including informed refusal of surgery
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the central ethical/legal reality. RN role: respect autonomy + ensure informed refusal + advocacy.

opts.text: Need to assess DECISION-MAKING CAPACITY (understands + appreciates + reasons + communicates a stable choice) — and recognize that pain, fear, or disagreement with the team does NOT by itself negate capacity
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — capacity assessment is essential and must not be conflated with disagreement. RN role: assess capacity + document.

opts.text: Maximize INTRAUTERINE RESUSCITATION the patient accepts — reposition (left lateral/hands-knees), STOP oxytocin/treat tachysystole, IV fluid bolus, maternal O2, vaginal exam to rule out cord prolapse — to improve fetal oxygenation and buy time
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — acceptable measures that protect the fetus without overriding refusal. RN role: implement promptly + reassess.

opts.text: Explore the REASON behind the refusal — here a prior traumatic, poorly-communicated cesarean and fear; addressing the underlying concern (trauma-informed, well-communicated, family-centered approach) may resolve the impasse without coercion
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — eliciting/addressing the 'why' is often the key to resolution. RN role: therapeutic communication + trauma-informed care.

opts.text: Activate the ETHICS process + multidisciplinary support (OB, ethics consult, chaplain, social work, support person) and ensure a professional interpreter is available if needed
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — ethics + team + interpreter support the patient and the process. RN role: mobilize resources.

opts.text: Document objectively — capacity assessment, information disclosed, interventions, ethics involvement, and the patient's decision in her own words; keep the door open to reconsideration
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — objective documentation + reversibility. RN role: document facts not judgments; support reconsideration.

opts.text: Allergies: NKDA — document and proceed
opts.rat: Allergy documentation. Relevant if surgery/medications proceed. Not the priority cue.

opts.text: Because the fetus is at risk, immediately obtain a court order to compel the cesarean and prepare to proceed over the patient's objection
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG + unethical/illegal. ACOG opposes court-ordered/forced cesareans on competent adults; forcing surgery is battery and the fetus's interests do not override the mother's bodily autonomy. The response is information + support, not coercion.

opts.text: Tell the patient that if she refuses, you will have to call child protective services and that she could lose custody — to motivate her to consent
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — this is coercion by threat, which is unethical and destroys trust. Refusal of intervention by a competent adult is not child abuse/neglect. Use honest information + addressing her concerns, never threats.

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.inst: Select ALL accurate linkages between cues and underlying principles.

opts.text: A competent (decisional) pregnant adult has the right to informed REFUSAL of intervention on her own body; autonomy + bodily integrity govern, and coercion/forced/court-ordered cesarean is unethical (ACOG) and constitutes battery
opts.rat: CORRECT — the core ethical/legal principle. Foundation for the entire response.

opts.text: Decision-making CAPACITY = understand + appreciate + reason + communicate a stable choice; it is presumed in adults and decision-specific; disagreement, pain, or fear does NOT negate capacity (assess for true impairments like intoxication/delirium/psychosis)
opts.rat: CORRECT — capacity framework. Foundation for honoring informed refusal.

opts.text: Category III tracing (absent variability + recurrent late decels) predicts abnormal fetal acid-base status → prompt intrauterine resuscitation and expedited delivery if unresolved; recurrent late decels = uteroplacental insufficiency
opts.rat: CORRECT — FHR interpretation framework. Foundation for the clinical urgency communicated to the patient.

opts.text: Intrauterine resuscitation (reposition, stop oxytocin/treat tachysystole, IV bolus, treat hypotension, O2, rule out cord prolapse, amnioinfusion for variables) improves fetal oxygenation and can be maximized as acceptable care while decisions are made
opts.rat: CORRECT — intrauterine resuscitation framework. Foundation for protecting the fetus without coercion.

opts.text: Informed consent/refusal requires disclosure + voluntariness + a professional interpreter for language barriers (never family/ad hoc) + documentation; addressing the underlying reason for refusal (e.g., birth trauma) is central to resolution
opts.rat: CORRECT — informed consent elements + therapeutic approach. Foundation for ethical communication.

opts.text: The standard for overriding a PARENT's refusal of treatment for a CHILD (best-interest, court-orderable) is DIFFERENT from a competent adult refusing for her own body; emergency/implied consent applies only when a patient has LOST capacity
opts.rat: CORRECT — distinguishes this from pediatric refusal (see [[case-080]]) and from emergency consent. Foundation for applying the right standard.

opts.text: Because pregnancy creates a second patient (the fetus), the mother's right to refuse is automatically forfeited and the team may proceed in the fetus's best interest
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. The fetus's interests do NOT legally override a competent mother's bodily autonomy; she does not forfeit her right to refuse. ACOG opposes coercion/forced cesarean. The response is information + support, not override.

opts.text: A patient who refuses a strongly recommended cesarean is by definition lacking capacity and should be declared incompetent so the team can consent on her behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Disagreeing with a recommendation is NOT evidence of incapacity. Capacity must be assessed by its actual criteria; an unwise choice by a capable adult is still her right.

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Rank the nursing priorities in this situation
steps.inst: Rank these nursing concerns from #1 (HIGHEST priority) to #5 (LOWEST).

opts.text: FETAL OXYGENATION via intrauterine resuscitation r/t Category III tracing — reposition, stop oxytocin/treat tachysystole, IV bolus, O2, rule out cord prolapse, continuous monitoring; maximize every measure the patient accepts while keeping the OR ready
opts.rat: #1 — HIGHEST. Immediate, acceptable measures that protect the fetus without overriding autonomy address the clinical urgency now. RN role: rapid intrauterine resuscitation + monitoring.

opts.text: ENSURE TRULY INFORMED DECISION + assess capacity r/t the refusal — verify understanding of diagnosis/recommendation/risks/alternatives/consequences (interpreter if needed), assess capacity, ensure voluntariness
opts.rat: #2 — HIGH. A valid decision (consent OR refusal) depends on it being informed + capacitated. RN role: capacity assessment + informed dialogue.

opts.text: THERAPEUTIC COMMUNICATION + address the underlying reason r/t prior birth trauma/fear — explore the 'why,' validate, offer trauma-informed/family-centered options; this often resolves the impasse without coercion
opts.rat: #3 — HIGH. Addressing the root concern is frequently the key to a voluntary resolution. RN role: trauma-informed therapeutic communication.

opts.text: ADVOCACY + ETHICS PROCESS r/t respecting autonomy — activate ethics consult + chaplain + social work + support person, protect the patient from coercion, and document objectively; keep the door open
opts.rat: #4 — Significant. Ethics + advocacy safeguard the patient's rights and the team's process. RN role: mobilize ethics/support + document + prevent coercion.

opts.text: TEAM SUPPORT + moral-distress debrief + trauma-informed postpartum planning r/t the emotional weight of the situation for staff and the patient's birth-experience repair
opts.rat: #5 — Important throughout + afterward. Supports staff wellbeing + the patient's longer-term recovery. RN role: facilitate debrief + plan trauma-informed follow-up.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH action, classify as INDICATED or NOT INDICATED in this refusal situation.

opts.text: Implement intrauterine resuscitation the patient accepts (reposition left lateral/hands-knees, stop oxytocin, IV bolus, maternal O2, vaginal exam to rule out cord prolapse) with continuous monitoring + OR on standby
opts.rat: Protects fetal oxygenation without overriding autonomy + buys time. RN role: implement + reassess.

opts.text: Assess decision-making capacity and ensure the refusal is truly informed (clear disclosure of risks/benefits/alternatives/consequences; professional interpreter if any barrier)
opts.rat: Essential to a valid decision. RN role: assess capacity + ensure informed dialogue + document.

opts.text: Explore and address the reason for refusal (prior traumatic cesarean/fear) and offer trauma-informed, family-centered options (narration, support person, chaplain/doula, regional anesthesia, gentle technique)
opts.rat: Addressing the root concern often resolves the impasse without coercion. RN role: therapeutic, trauma-informed communication.

opts.text: Activate an ethics consult and engage chaplain, social work, the OB team, and the patient's support person; provide a professional interpreter as needed
opts.rat: Ethics + multidisciplinary support the patient and process. RN role: mobilize resources.

opts.text: Stay present and non-judgmental, keep the door open to reconsideration, and proceed promptly with a trauma-informed cesarean if/when the patient gives informed consent
opts.rat: Preserves the therapeutic alliance + respects reversibility + enables timely care if she consents. RN role: presence + responsiveness.

opts.text: Document objectively — capacity assessment, information disclosed, interventions, ethics involvement, and the patient's decision in her own words
opts.rat: Accurate, objective documentation is essential. RN role: document facts, not judgments.

opts.text: Obtain an emergency court order to compel the cesarean and proceed over the patient's objection because the fetus is at risk
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG/unethical. ACOG opposes court-ordered/forced cesareans on competent adults; forcing surgery is battery; the fetus does not override maternal bodily autonomy.

opts.text: Threaten a child-welfare/CPS referral or loss of custody to pressure the patient into consenting
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Coercion by threat is unethical and destroys trust; refusal of intervention by a competent adult is not abuse/neglect.

opts.text: Declare the patient incompetent because she is refusing a clearly recommended, potentially life-saving intervention, then consent on her behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Disagreement is not incapacity. Capacity must be assessed by its real criteria; a capable adult's unwise choice is still her right.

opts.text: Withdraw from the bedside / reduce attentiveness to express disapproval of her choice and let her 'face the consequences'
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG — abandonment/punishment is unethical. The nurse must remain present, supportive, and non-judgmental regardless of the patient's choice.

opts.text: Use the patient's husband to interpret the medical risks quickly so the conversation moves faster
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. A professional interpreter (not family/ad hoc) must be used for any language barrier to ensure accurate, unbiased disclosure (she is English-proficient here, but Igbo interpreter is available if needed).

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Action sequence at the moment of refusal
steps.inst: Rank the order in which the RN should act. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Immediately implement intrauterine resuscitation she accepts (reposition, stop oxytocin, IV bolus, O2, vaginal exam to rule out cord prolapse) + continuous monitoring + notify OB + ready the OR
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Address the fetal-oxygenation urgency now with acceptable measures while decisions are made. RN role: rapid resuscitation + escalation.

opts.text: Assess capacity + ensure the refusal is truly informed — clearly disclose diagnosis, recommendation, risks/benefits, alternatives, and consequences (interpreter if needed); confirm understanding
opts.rat: #2 — Second. A valid decision requires capacity + information. RN role: capacity assessment + informed dialogue.

opts.text: Explore and address the underlying reason for refusal with trauma-informed therapeutic communication; offer family-centered, well-communicated options
opts.rat: #3 — Third. Addressing the root concern is often what resolves the impasse. RN role: trauma-informed communication.

opts.text: Activate the ethics consult + multidisciplinary/support resources (OB, ethics, chaplain, social work, support person); avoid all coercion; document objectively
opts.rat: #4 — Fourth. Ethics + advocacy + documentation safeguard the patient and process. RN role: mobilize resources + protect from coercion.

opts.text: Keep the door open to reconsideration and proceed promptly with a trauma-informed cesarean if/when she gives informed consent; plan birth-experience debrief + team support
opts.rat: #5 — Last/ongoing. Respect reversibility + enable timely care if consent is given + support recovery and staff. RN role: responsiveness + follow-up.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action taken, classify as Appropriate or Inappropriate.

opts.text: The nurse maximized intrauterine resuscitation the patient accepted (repositioning, stopped oxytocin, IV bolus, O2, ruled out cord prolapse), kept continuous monitoring, and kept the OR on standby
opts.rat: Protects fetal oxygenation without overriding autonomy + maintains readiness. Model handling.

opts.text: The team assessed capacity, found it intact, clearly disclosed the risks/alternatives, and respected the informed refusal as a valid decision of a competent adult
opts.rat: Correct application of capacity + informed-refusal principles. Respects autonomy.

opts.text: The OB and nurse explored her prior birth trauma, validated her fear, and committed to a supportive, well-communicated, trauma-informed cesarean — after which she voluntarily consented
opts.rat: Addressing the underlying concern resolved the impasse without coercion + led to a voluntary, informed decision. Ideal handling.

opts.text: An ethics consult was activated to support the patient's autonomy and the team's process, with chaplain, social work, and her support person engaged
opts.rat: Ethics + multidisciplinary support is appropriate (to support, not override). Model handling.

opts.text: The team obtained an emergency court order and proceeded with a forced cesarean over the patient's objection while she still had capacity
opts.rat: ⚠️ Unethical/illegal — forced/court-ordered cesarean on a competent adult is opposed by ACOG and constitutes battery. Never appropriate for a decisional refusing adult.

opts.text: A nurse told the patient she would be reported to child protective services and could lose custody if she did not consent
opts.rat: ⚠️ Coercion by threat — unethical, destroys trust, and is not warranted (refusal by a competent adult is not abuse/neglect).

opts.text: Because she initially refused, the team documented her as 'incompetent and noncompliant' and consented on her behalf
opts.rat: ⚠️ Disagreement is not incapacity; labeling/overriding her is wrong + the documentation is judgmental and inaccurate. Capacity must be properly assessed.

opts.text: After consent, the team performed a trauma-informed, family-centered cesarean (husband present, narration, regional anesthesia), supported skin-to-skin + breastfeeding, and planned a birth-experience debrief
opts.rat: Trauma-informed, family-centered care + bonding support + follow-up debrief — excellent handling that repairs the birth experience.

opts.text: Documentation captured the capacity assessment, information disclosed, interventions, ethics involvement, and the patient's decision in her own words — facts, not judgments
opts.rat: Objective, complete documentation is exactly right. Protects the patient and the team.

opts.text: The nurse stepped away from the bedside and limited interaction to show disapproval of the patient's initial refusal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Abandonment/punishment is unethical; the nurse must remain present, supportive, and non-judgmental regardless of the patient's choice.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
